summary: Tell-Don't-Ask is a principle that encourages moving behavior into objects rather than extracting data and acting on it externally. The principle helps programmers remember that object-orientation bundles data with functions that operate on that data. While many find it useful for improving code design by co-locating tightly coupled elements, Fowler personally doesn't emphasize it because it can lead to extreme positions like eliminating all query methods. He notes that sometimes objects collaborate effectively by providing information, and good design requires balancing multiple trade-offs rather than following any single principle dogmatically.
key_points: Tell-Don't-Ask means telling objects what to do rather than asking for their data and acting on it externally
The principle promotes co-locating data and behavior because they are tightly coupled - changes in one cause changes in the other
Taken too far, the principle can lead to 'Getter Eradicators' who eliminate all query methods unnecessarily
Sometimes objects collaborate more effectively by providing information through queries
Good design involves trade-offs, and co-locating data and behavior is just one factor among many
money_quotes: Tell-Don't-Ask is a principle that helps people remember that object-orientation is about bundling data with the functions that operate on that data.
Things that are tightly coupled should be in the same component.
But personally, I don't use tell-dont-ask.
I've seen it encourage people to become GetterEradicators, seeking to get rid of all query methods.
There are times when objects collaborate effectively by providing information.
I've seen code get into convolutions of only telling where suitably responsible query methods would simplify matters.
Good design is all about trade-offs, and co-locating data and behavior is just one factor to bear in mind.
key_insight: While Tell-Don't-Ask usefully reminds us to co-locate data and behavior, treating it as an absolute rule leads to unnecessarily convoluted code - good design requires balancing multiple trade-offs.
